posts.hook: Smooth pursuit is the eye movement system that tracks moving objects. It's different from saccades — and most adults have never deliberately trained it.
posts.caption:
Two types of voluntary eye movement:

1. Saccades — fast, ballistic jumps between fixed points (reading)
2. Smooth pursuit — slow, continuous tracking of a moving target

Smooth pursuit is the system athletes train.

Baseball batters. Tennis players. Surgeons. Fighter pilots.

Figure-8 tracking is the classic smooth pursuit exercise:
→ Trace a horizontal figure-8 with your eyes
→ Keep head still
→ Move as slowly as possible without the eye 'slipping'

3 minutes. No equipment.

Guided version: axeo.vision/exercise

#SmoothPursuit #Figure8Tracking #EyeExercise #Axeo #HealthTok #EyeHealth #SportsVision

posts.hook: Figure-8 tracking: why this specific eye movement pattern trains the smooth pursuit system — and who uses it.
posts.caption:
Smooth pursuit is your eye's ability to follow a moving target without losing lock.

It's the most metabolically demanding eye movement — it requires constant motor correction.

Measures of smooth pursuit quality:
→ Gain: how accurately eye velocity matches target velocity (1.0 = perfect)
→ Catch-up saccades: little corrective jumps when pursuit slips — more = worse
→ Phase lag: how far behind your eye trails the target

Figure-8 tracking exercises smooth pursuit by:
1. Requiring continuous directional changes (unlike simple horizontal tracking)
2. Challenging the pursuit system in all quadrants
3. Demanding near-zero catch-up saccades if done correctly

How to do it:
→ Fix a point on the wall or use a finger held at arm's length
→ Trace a horizontal figure-8 with eyes only — head still
→ Go as slowly as you can manage without your eye slipping
→ 3 minutes. 3 sets.

Alternative: use a slow-moving on-screen dot — Axeo's exercise page has this.

Guided version: axeo.vision/exercise

#SmoothPursuit #Figure8Tracking #EyeExercise #Axeo #AxeoVision #SportsVision #EyeHealth

posts.hook: Figure-8 tracking: 3 minutes to train the smooth pursuit system used by elite athletes.
posts.caption:
Smooth pursuit = your eye's ability to track a continuously moving target.

Figure-8 tracking is how athletes train it.

Why the figure-8 specifically:
→ Constant direction changes challenge all quadrants
→ Forces continuous motor correction
→ The slowest you can go = the hardest it works

How to do it:
1. Fix a point at arm's length (or use Axeo's moving dot)
2. Trace a horizontal figure-8 with eyes only
3. Head completely still
4. Go as slowly as possible
5. 3 sets, 3 minutes each

Benefits: better sports tracking, reduced oculomotor fatigue, improved reading

Guided at axeo.vision/exercise

posts.hook: The eye exercise elite athletes use that nobody in mainstream fitness talks about — figure-8 tracking.
posts.caption:
Sports vision training has been used by professional athletes for decades.

Key targets:
→ Reaction time improvement
→ Ball tracking accuracy
→ Peripheral awareness
→ Depth perception under pressure

All of these rely on smooth pursuit quality — your eye's ability to track a moving object without slipping.

Studies on MLB batters, tennis players, and NFL receivers show measurable smooth pursuit performance above population norms. This isn't genetics — it's trained.

The most accessible smooth pursuit exercise: figure-8 tracking.

Trace a horizontal figure-8 with your eyes. Head still. As slowly as you can manage. 3 sets.

What you're training:
→ Sustained smooth pursuit gain
→ Pursuit in all four quadrants
→ Minimizing catch-up saccades

You don't need to be an athlete to benefit. Better smooth pursuit = better reading, driving, and reduced oculomotor fatigue.

Free guided version: axeo.vision/exercise

posts.hook: Smooth pursuit is one of the most studied eye movement systems in neuroscience — and you can train it with a piece of string or a moving dot. Here's what the research shows:
posts.caption:
1/ Smooth pursuit: the eye movement system that tracks a continuously moving target by velocity-matching it. Unlike saccades (which are fast ballistic jumps), smooth pursuit is a continuously updated closed-loop motor control process.

2/ What determines smooth pursuit quality: Gain (how well eye velocity matches target velocity — 1.0 is perfect), phase lag (how far the eye trails the target), and catch-up saccade frequency (corrective jumps the system uses when pursuit slips — more = worse).

3/ Why athletes train it: studies on professional baseball batters, tennis players, and combat pilots consistently show superior smooth pursuit gain and lower catch-up saccade rates compared to matched non-athletes. This is not genetics — training studies show this improves with specific practice.

4/ The figure-8 exercise: trace a horizontal figure-8 (infinity symbol) with your eyes, keeping your head still, moving as slowly as possible. The horizontal figure-8 is preferred over simple horizontal tracking because it requires continuous directional changes across all four visual quadrants.

5/ The 'slower is harder' principle: smooth pursuit performance degrades at very low speeds (below 1°/sec) because the system cannot maintain smooth motion without velocity feedback. Going deliberately slow maximizes neuromotor demand.

6/ Axeo has a guided figure-8 tracking exercise at axeo.vision/exercise — uses a slow-moving on-screen dot for reference. Free, browser-based. The full Week 4 exercise library is there: 20-20-20, palming, focus shifting, Brock String, eye rotations, and figure-8.

Two more days to lock in the routine before next week.
posts.visual_notes: Scientific thread. Smooth pursuit gain metrics. Training principle explained. Credibility through neuroscience framing. End with Axeo library callout.

posts.hook: What sports vision science tells us about oculomotor training — and why it matters beyond athletics.
posts.caption:
Sports vision programs have been integrated into professional athletics training for decades.

The core insight: visual performance is trainable — not just correctable with lenses.

Key trainable skills:
→ Smooth pursuit accuracy (tracking velocity)
→ Saccadic precision (reading, scanning)
→ Vergence range (near and far coordination)
→ Accommodation speed (focus switching)
→ Visual reaction time

These skills are not just relevant for athletes. They matter for:
→ Surgeons (steady tracking during procedures)
→ Pilots and drivers (environmental scanning)
→ Knowledge workers (prolonged reading accuracy)
→ Aging adults (fall prevention — gaze stabilization)

The research trajectory is clear: oculomotor training improves measurable performance in non-athlete populations too.

The barrier has always been access — vision therapy is expensive, specialized, and under-prescribed.

Axeo is building a free, accessible oculomotor training library. Week 4 covers six core exercises: 20-20-20 rule, palming, focus shifting, Brock String, eye rotations, and figure-8 tracking.

All free at axeo.vision/exercise. No signup. Any device.

The clinical knowledge is there. The tools should be too.

#EyeHealth #VisionTraining #SportsScience #WorkplaceHealth #DigitalHealth #Optometry #EyeExercise
